title: Cost, tax, and drag — the hidden variables
subtitle: Two portfolios with identical asset allocations and gross returns can have dramatically different net outcomes depending on costs and tax efficiency.

body.content: Gross return is what an investment earns before any deductions. Net return is what you actually keep. The gap between them — costs, taxes, and behavioral drag — is where most investors lose value they never knew they were losing.

body.label: The compounding cost of fees
body.content: A 1% annual management fee sounds trivial. Over 30 years, on a $200,000 portfolio returning 7% gross, the difference between a 0.1% fee and a 1% fee is approximately $180,000 in final portfolio value. Fees compound. The money you pay in fees is money that does not compound for you — for the entirety of your investment horizon.

items: Expense ratios: the annual fee charged by funds, expressed as a percentage of assets. Index funds typically charge 0.03–0.20%. Active funds often charge 0.5–1.5%.
Transaction costs: brokerage commissions and bid-ask spreads. Less relevant for long-term buy-and-hold strategies.
Tax drag: taxes on dividends and realized capital gains reduce compounding. Tax-inefficient funds in taxable accounts are a persistent drag.
Behavioral drag: the return lost to poor timing decisions — buying high, selling low. This is often the largest cost of all, and the hardest to measure.

body.content: Tax efficiency is about location as much as selection. Placing tax-inefficient assets (high-dividend stocks, REITs, taxable bonds) in tax-advantaged accounts and holding tax-efficient assets (index funds, growth equities) in taxable accounts is a high-leverage, low-effort optimization.

You cannot control market returns. You can control costs, tax efficiency, and your own behavior. These three levers, consistently applied over decades, matter more than most investment selection decisions.
